event: Old Norse annals record "Svalbarð" ("cold edge"), a name some scholars link to a sighting of the archipelago, though it may refer to other Arctic lands.
Dutch explorer Willem Barentsz, searching for the Northeast Passage, sights and charts Spitsbergen, marking the verifiable European discovery of Svalbard.
Henry Hudson explores the region and reports abundant whales, drawing European whalers toward the waters around Svalbard.
English and Dutch whaling expeditions begin large-scale commercial hunting of bowhead whales off Spitsbergen.
Dutch whalers establish the seasonal whaling station of Smeerenburg on Amsterdam Island in northwestern Spitsbergen.
Jan Mayen is charted and named after Dutch whaling captain Jan Jacobszoon May, who used it as a base for Arctic whaling.
Dutch whalers attempt the first recorded overwintering on Jan Mayen, but all members of the party perish from scurvy.
Seven Dutch volunteers overwinter at Smeerenburg on Spitsbergen, but the entire group dies of scurvy by spring.
Friedrich Martens publishes an influential account of Spitsbergen, documenting its whaling industry, wildlife, and geography.
Russian Pomor hunters from the White Sea region begin overwintering on Svalbard to trap walrus, seals, polar bears, and Arctic foxes.
Russian Pomor sailor Aleksei Khimkov and three companions survive a celebrated six-year castaway ordeal on Edgeøya in eastern Svalbard.
Commercial whaling around Svalbard collapses as bowhead whale stocks become severely depleted after nearly two centuries of hunting.
Norwegian hunters increasingly replace Russian Pomors in trapping and overwintering across the archipelago.
Norwegian geologist Baltazar Mathias Keilhau conducts the first scientific expedition to Svalbard by a Norwegian.
French scientific expeditions aboard La Recherche carry out extensive surveys of Spitsbergen's geology and natural history.
Swedish geologist Otto Torell leads major scientific expeditions that advance the geological mapping of Svalbard.
Adolf Erik Nordenskiold's expedition is forced into a difficult overwintering at Mosselbukta in northern Spitsbergen.
Sweden establishes a research station at Kapp Thordsen for the First International Polar Year.
Tourism begins as a hotel is built at Hotellneset near present-day Longyearbyen to receive Arctic cruise visitors.
Salomon August Andree launches his ill-fated balloon expedition toward the North Pole from Danskoya in northwest Spitsbergen.
Norwegian skipper Soren Zachariassen extracts and ships the first commercial cargo of Svalbard coal.
American businessman John Munro Longyear founds the Arctic Coal Company, beginning industrial coal mining and establishing the settlement that becomes Longyearbyen.
The Norwegian company Store Norske Spitsbergen Kulkompani is founded, taking over major coal operations on the archipelago.
The Spitsbergen Treaty is signed in Paris, recognizing Norwegian sovereignty over Svalbard while granting signatory nations equal rights to economic activity.
The Svalbard Act takes effect, formally placing the archipelago under Norwegian sovereignty and administration as part of Norway.
Roald Amundsen, Lincoln Ellsworth, and Umberto Nobile depart Ny-Alesund aboard the airship Norge on the first verified flight over the North Pole.
Norway formally annexes Jan Mayen, which becomes part of the Kingdom of Norway.
Norway establishes a permanent meteorological station on Jan Mayen.
Soviet trust Arktikugol begins operating coal mines at the Russian settlements of Barentsburg, Grumant, and Pyramiden.
Allied forces evacuate the population of Svalbard and destroy mining infrastructure to deny resources to Nazi Germany during World War II.
German warships, including the battleship Tirpitz, bombard Longyearbyen and Barentsburg in Operation Zitronella.
Germany maintains the secret weather station Haudegen in northern Svalbard, whose crew surrenders in September 1945, among the last German forces to capitulate.
Norway joins NATO, reinforcing the strategic importance of the demilitarized Svalbard archipelago in the early Cold War.
Svalbard Airport area developments and improved air links begin connecting the archipelago more regularly to the Norwegian mainland.
Norway establishes large national parks and nature reserves, protecting a major share of Svalbard's territory and wildlife.
Svalbard Airport, Longyear opens, providing the first year-round airport and ending the archipelago's seasonal isolation.
The University Centre in Svalbard (UNIS) is established in Longyearbyen, becoming the world's northernmost institution of higher education.
Longyearbyen gains a locally elected community council, introducing a measure of local self-government.
Pyramiden, the Russian mining town abandoned in 1998, begins reopening seasonally as a tourist and heritage site.
The Svalbard Global Seed Vault opens near Longyearbyen to safeguard duplicates of the world's crop seeds against catastrophe.
An avalanche strikes Longyearbyen homes, prompting later relocations and new measures to manage climate-driven hazards.
Norway opens the Arctic World Archive in a former mine near Longyearbyen to preserve digital data for posterity.
Store Norske begins closing its last coal mine, Gruve 7, as Svalbard transitions away from coal toward renewable energy and research.
